does: This paper introduces the concept of omissive bias—the systematic absence of religious frameworks in LLM responses to ethical questions—and proposes a measurement framework to quantify it. The authors develop the AllFaith Religious Representation Benchmark, comprising 150 everyday ethical questions sourced from real chat transcripts and faith communities, covering topics like grief, forgiveness, and relationships. They employ an LLM-as-judge rubric that credits responses for any mention of religion, religious practices, or religious leaders. The benchmark evaluates whether state-of-the-art LLMs invoke religious perspectives when addressing personally and morally salient questions, finding evidence that these systems systematically underrepresent religious reasoning compared to what might be expected given religion's historical and contemporary role in ethical deliberation.
connects: This work builds on growing research into value alignment and bias in LLMs, extending beyond studies that measure overt political leanings or social stereotypes to examine what LLMs omit rather than what they erroneously include. It responds to concerns raised in broader alignment literature about whether LLMs adequately represent human value pluralism and diverse reasoning frameworks. The paper also engages with long-standing work on representational bias in AI systems, while bringing attention to a previously underexamined dimension: the absence of religious and spiritual perspectives that have historically structured moral reasoning across cultures. It complements existing fairness and bias benchmarks by reframing bias detection as encompassing gaps in representation.
enables: This work establishes omissive bias as a measurable and actionable category for LLM evaluation, opening pathways for developing more pluralistic AI systems. Future research can apply similar frameworks to quantify absence-based biases across other value systems and reasoning traditions. The benchmark enables system developers to audit their models for religious representation gaps and explore whether and how to integrate diverse ethical frameworks more equitably. It also points toward broader questions about whose knowledge and reasoning practices are privileged or marginalized in training data and model outputs. The approach suggests methods for ensuring LLMs serve diverse users by reflecting the full spectrum of reasoning traditions people draw upon when facing existential and moral questions.
